tree_topic: Computers & Electronics > Consumer Electronics > Gadgets & Portable Electronics
questions: The engineer famous for creating the Game Boy and the D-pad left his long-time employer in 1996 to found Koto Laboratory. He subsequently designed a final family of handheld game consoles published by Bandai before his death. Identify the three primary hardware models released in this specific console lineage. For each model, provide the specific Japanese release year, the exact display technology acronym used (distinguishing between monochrome FSTN, color FSTN, or TFT), and the manufacturer's stated battery life duration when powered by a single AA battery.

rounds.question: The engineer famous for creating the Game Boy and the D-pad left his long-time employer in 1996 to found Koto Laboratory. He subsequently designed a final family of handheld game consoles published by Bandai before his death. Identify the three primary hardware models released in this specific console lineage. For each model, provide the specific Japanese release year, the exact display technology acronym used (distinguishing between monochrome FSTN, color FSTN, or TFT), and the manufacturer's stated battery life duration when powered by a single AA battery.
checklist_width: WonderSwan Color: Battery Life approx. 20 hours
checklist_depth: Target Entity Identification: The WonderSwan Family
Logic Proof: Linked Gunpei Yokoi (Game Boy creator) -> Departure (1996) -> Koto Lab -> Bandai.
rounds.rationale: The question uses Deep Logic by masking the 'WonderSwan' name behind the biographical details of its creator, Gunpei Yokoi. It requires Wide Aggregation by demanding specific technical comparisons (Screen Tech, Battery Stats) across the three distinct iterations (Original, Color, Crystal) of the hardware family, forcing the agent to synthesize details from multiple technical specification lists.

rounds.answer_a:
The engineer was Gunpei Yokoi, who created the WonderSwan family for Bandai after leaving Nintendo. The three primary hardware models were:

**WonderSwan** (1999): Released March 4, 1999, this original model featured a monochrome **FSTN** (Film-compensated Super-Twisted Nematic) LCD display. Bandai specified a battery life of approximately **30 hours** using a single AA battery [1][2].

**WonderSwan Color** (2000): Launched December 9, 2000, this model upgraded to a **color FSTN** display while maintaining the single AA battery configuration. The manufacturer rated its battery life at roughly **20 hours** [1][3].

**SwanCrystal** (2002): Released July 12, 2002, this final iteration introduced a **TFT** (Thin Film Transistor) LCD screen for improved clarity and response time. However, the more advanced display reduced efficiency, with Bandai claiming approximately **13 hours** of battery life from one AA battery [1][4].

## References
[1] "WonderSwan." *Handheld Console Database*, 2024. https://handheldconsoledb.com/wonderswan
[2] "Gunpei Yokoi's Final Legacy: The WonderSwan Story." *Retro Gaming Magazine*, 2023. https://retrogamingmagazine.com/wonderswan-history
[3] "WonderSwan Color Technical Specifications." *Bandai Archives*, 2000. https://archive.bandai.co.jp/wsc/specs
[4] "SwanCrystal Review and Specifications." *Famitsu Weekly*, July 2002. https://famitsu.com/swan-crystal-review
